entry.title: New applicant workbench
entry.content:
please can someone confirm is the New Applicant Work Bench will be mandatory as of May 2026. 

There is an issue in production that the ability to send agencies emails is no longer working or coming up as an option.

We have asked SAP but no one can seem to confirm what is happening. Please can you advise before we spend time on having to change the process rules as a lot of the original business rules for legacy are no longer working and a lot of work needs to be allocated to fix this issue. Also please can someone advise when it will be possible to use the agency email functionality again as only works if toggled to legacy in QA but not in production.

entry.title: Re: How to Establish a Multi-Impact Environmental Footprint with SAP
entry.content: This is a vital shift in perspective, @stefan_kohlmeier! Moving beyond carbon to include water usage and resource depletion is essential for a truly holistic sustainability strategy. I'm particularly interested in how the "hidden hotspots" identified through multi-impact analysis might reveal operational inefficiencies that a standard carbon-only audit would miss. This course looks like a great next step for those of us already working with SAP Sustainability Footprint Management.

entry.title: Re: Crystal Reports 2016 Free Trial Product Key?
entry.content: I understand how frustrating that loop can be. Usually, the trial key for Crystal Reports is sent via an automated email immediately after registration; if it's not in your spam folder, it often indicates a sync issue with the SAP Store profile. Since store.admin@sap.com is unmonitored, you might try re-triggering the "Start Trial" button from a different browser or private window while logged in, as this sometimes forces the license generation to refresh on your dashboard. Good luck getting the installation finished!

entry.title: Re: Snote Implementation April 2026, 3727345, 3701228, 37...
entry.content:
Hi @anandhusap, for this specific set of notes related to the Income Tax Bill 2025 (India Payroll), the manual steps are crucial. After implementing 3701228 and 3727345, you must check transaction PE01 to verify if your payroll schema (typically IN00) requires manual synchronization or if any sub-schemas were marked for modification in the note's side-car documentation.

Also, pay close attention to table V_T7INI10_EXTRACT via SM30; there is a known dependency where this object might appear as "unknown" if the notes are not activated in the correct sequence. For those of us moving toward a "Clean Core," I’d also recommend using SAP Cloud ALM to orchestrate the automated testing of these schema changes to ensure no custom logistics or payroll logic was disrupted.

entry.title: Re: ABAP Compare (Sorted Class Methods) in Eclipse has a bug
entry.content: @Hismayilov I have just checked in my system, it works fine for me, TYPES don't appear in the implementation part, they appear in the definition part. My system: ABAP 7.58 SP 1, Eclipse 2025-09, ADT 3.52.3.

entry.title: Joule AI Custom Solution for Material Shortage and Fulfillment
entry.content:
A few weeks ago, I had the opportunity to participate in a hackathon to build a Joule AI solution that addresses material shortages in an expedited manner. This can help Inventory Management users save time and money.

I am sure many SAP customers have similar requirements, and I want to share my experience here so it can be beneficial to others.

Problem Statement: IM users have to manually check shortages in SAP applications and create purchase requisitions if there is any shortage, to make sure production runs smoothly. Users perform this activity in the backend S/4HANA system.

Solution Architecture & Design: I built the artifacts below as part of this Joule project to address the requirement.

  1. Actions to get material shortages and create purchase requisitions.
  2. Skills to call these APIs and perform tasks.
  3. An agent that can process user requests.
  4. A test environment to deploy and test the project.

A destination to the backend system already exists to leverage the APIs and to create and test actions.

Actions built in SAP Build:

The action below was created to read material shortages from the backend by leveraging the Material Coverage API.

I also created a material input variable to filter the API using the material number. You can adjust the input and output as needed and test them in this section.

A condition is applied to map the user input material to the API material attribute in the C_MaterialCoverage entity.

Like the Material Coverage API, I configured another action to create a purchase requisition in the backend system. Please remember to enable CSRF protection (if applicable) for that POST API call.

Now let’s go into the Joule Studio to build the Joule project. As you can see from the screenshot below, a couple of Joule skills and an agent have been created.

This Joule skill is responsible for creating purchase requisition in the backend system.

Joule skills can also be consumed directly without an agent. You can enable or disable the functionality, as highlighted below.

These are the minimum inputs required to create a purchase requisition. Of course, it depends on how the backend is configured for a particular document type.

The purchase requisition number is the output from the skill.

Everything we’ve seen in the screenshots above is related to the Trigger part.

Below is the step where the corresponding Purchase Requisition action has been configured. A backend destination variable is also configured to create the purchase requisition in the corresponding backend system.

There are multiple other options available to use as well, like Process, Condition, etc.

The Trigger inputs are mapped to the Action inputs.

This is the output from the Action.

At the end the Action result is mapped to the Purchase Requisition variable which we are interested in.

Like the Purchase requisition skill, Material Shortage skill is also configured.

Now it’s time to configure the agent.

It’s important to set the expertise and instructions so the agent understands how to behave.

There are multiple LLM providers available in the Model Settings: OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, and Mistral AI.

You can select any provider and the LLM they offer for response generation and planning.

Once everything is done, release and deploy the application.

A new test environment has been created under Control Tower to deploy and test this project.

To launch the agent, navigate to the Joule tab and click Launch.

It’s testing time.

I entered a prompt to pull the shortage information for a product. As you can see in the screenshot below, the agent responded with the shortage details. This information is pulled from the backend system.

Next, I want to create a purchase requisition. The expectation was that the agent would reuse as much information as possible from the previous step and ask questions only if additional input was required.

As expected, the agent understood the request and figured out that some inputs were still missing to create the purchase requisition. It asked for those details to proceed.

Here you go. The requisition has been created in the backend.

You can check the log by clicking the highlighted button top right corner.

Conclusion: The Joule AI solution is flexible enough to understand user requests and process them. Users don't have to navigate through multiple screens to complete the job.

AI solutions are powerful because they make many processes simple and easy. They can support different scenarios depending on how the agent is configured. There are J4D, J4C, and other tools that can help developers, consultants, and businesses save a lot of time and add value.

Key Challenges: I found that date mapping is not easy in the formula editor, since the skill inputs are always strings.

Error handling is also challenging. The skill doesn’t provide a way to map action/API errors so the agent can guide the user to resolve the issue in the back end. For example: The Purchasing Organization doesn’t exist or authorization errors.

SAP Joule Studio is evolving, and I hope these gaps will be addressed soon.

Please feel free to comment if you have any questions. l will try to answer as much as I can.

Thank you!

entry.title: 3 Journeys That Redefine Your Logistics in a Disruptive World
entry.content:
The world of logistics is undergoing a profound transformation, driven by volatility, global fragmentation, ongoing supply chain disruptions, and rapid technological change. Workforce scarcity impacting global logistics operations, logistics corridors becoming tools in geopolitical conflicts, and continuously rising transportation costs all point to the same reality: Future-proofing your logistics is no longer a strategic choice, but the critical tipping point that separates those who will rise from those who will fall in an increasingly unforgiving global economy.   

Modernizing logistics is no longer about “if” but about “how fast” and “how far”. 

Traditional logistics systems were designed primarily for efficiency. Today’s supply chains, however, are more complex and exposed to even more risk, requiring resilience, agility, and smarter decision‑making across warehousing and transportation operations. 

Pressure is constant, and from multiple directions, including geopolitical tensions, economic volatility, stringent government regulations, and rising customer expectations. The core challenge is no longer reacting to individual disruptions but overcoming constant firefighting. Leaders are seeking a clear path toward a more agile and future‑ready logistics strategy. Two questions sit at the center of this challenge: how fast do logistics operations have to change, and how complex are the operations that must be supported? 

Answering these questions starts with examining the technological foundations that power your current logistics operations. Long-standing solutions like SAP's ECC LE-WM, LE-TRA (ERP Central Component Logistics Execution Warehouse and Transportation Management), and Business Suite EWM/TM (Extended Warehouse Management/Transportation Management) have served reliably for decades, yet SAP continues to evolve to serve today's demands for real-time adaptability and scale.  

SAP’s logistics portfolio, which has supported customers across three decades, continues to evolve with adaptive, cloud-native, AI-driven platforms capable of learning, predicting, and orchestrating logistics processes autonomously, because that’s what companies need to compete now and in the future. 

The right journey depends on innovation speed, risk tolerance, and operational complexity.​ 

Future‑proofing logistics ultimately comes down to choosing the right modernization path. Every organization has a unique starting point, depending on how existing solutions like ECC LE-TRA/LE-WM or the SAP Business Suite have been implemented across the business. To choose the right next step, leaders need to understand the available options, determine what fits with the organization’s appetite for innovation, and assess the transformational impact on the team. 

Gartner’s logistics complexity model, breaking down process complexity across 5 levels, can help when setting a transformation strategy.  

Companies operating at levels 1 to 3 of Gartner’s model are typically characterized by manual or semi-automated processes, regional distribution, and lower complexity. These companies are best-suited for SaaS-native warehouse and/or transportation solutions that offer the implementation speed of standardized workflows, cloud qualities such as flexible, mobile interface, and advantages from improved scalability and lower TCO.  

Organizations at levels 4 and 5 require warehouse automation for high volume distribution, multi-modal transportation, and orchestration across multiple ERP systems. These companies seek the benefit of dedicated cloud environments that offer a higher level of adaptation for more control over their operations and their solution landscape. 

SAP offers different pathways to help businesses run and modernize across the span of operational complexity. Below are three journeys to guide your strategic planning for modernization. Each journey can be aligned to your high-level business ambitions, accounting for process complexity, innovation speed, and risk tolerance. 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

Journey 1: Sustain Temporary Stability While You Prepare for Modernization 

Move your legacy ECC LE TRA/LE￼WM to S/4HANA Stockroom Management to keep your operations supported until 2040, giving you more time to manage your transformation. This journey is about sustaining what works until you need to evolve. It is designed for organizations that need more time to move their decades-old warehousing solution into the future. This approach, rooted in Stockroom Management, extends the life of a proven system without prematurely forcing change. It is a temporary but intentional holding pattern for conservative logistics strategies based on a firm foundation. Throughout this journey, SAP Logistics Management can be the go-to solution when you are ready to move from Stockroom Management. 

Journey 2a: Modernize Your Logistics in Controlled Steps   

For organizations that know modernization is essential but need to be mindful of organizational change management, this journey offers a stable path. It begins with moving on￼premise ECC LE-TRA/LE-WM to SAP S/4HANA Cloud, private edition, with basic Warehouse Management and Transportation Management capabilities, creating a modern foundation for future readiness. From there, companies can evolve their processes toward more advanced options as their needs grow (see 2b). This journey is best suited to businesses that want flexibility and control in their operations as well as in their change management. This journey also supports deeply integrated logistics that align with your RISE journey.  

Along this journey, if you find that your business complexity is manageable, SAP Logistics Management can even be incorporated as the most modern, AI-native SAP solution for logistics, to complement your ongoing operations.   

Journey 2b: Move Your large-scale Logistics to the cloud   

This journey supports organizations that need to manage highly specific processes, high-automation, and mission-critical logistics execution with one or multiple ERP systems. You can migrate from Business Suite EWM/TM to the cloud with S/4HANA Cloud Advanced EWM/TM without losing depth or control. This path can also serve as a second step for advancing operations from S/4HANA Cloud EWM/TM basic to S/4HANA Cloud EWM/TM advanced, to support your growing business needs.  

Journey 3: The Modernization Game-changer for Your Logistics  

For innovation leaders ready to sprint ahead of the competition, this journey represents the direct path to the most modern logistics operating model. Companies move from on-premise ECC LE-TRA/LE-WM to AI-native SAP Logistics Management bringing together cloud delivery, embedded intelligence, and network connectivity in a single solution for warehousing and logistics. This is the right path for businesses that want to standardize faster, simplify their landscape, and take advantage of continuous innovation. You gain a more connected and adaptive logistics model with built-in AI and a carrier network. Along this journey, you are setting the pace for modern supply chains.

The future of logistics is cloud-first, AI at the core, and modular by design. 

Logistics is accelerating into a new era, one defined not only by efficiency, but by intelligent adaptability and autonomous orchestration. SAP’s strategy, embodied in its evolving logistics portfolio, offers a clear, flexible, and future-ready path for companies at every level of complexity. 

Whether maintaining existing operations, maturing gradually as complexity increases, or leapfrogging to leading-edge capabilities, you can now compose and run your entire logistics operations on SAP. Basic to moderate complexity processes are supported with SAP Logistics Management and advanced and highly automated business with S/4HANA Cloud EWM and TM. By combining the two, you can design and achieve logistics networks to improve speed, agility, and resilience. 

Want to explore which logistics journey fits your business best? Don’t miss our Future of Supply Chain conversation with SAP’s Till Dengel: Logistics in the 21st Century: The Journey to the Cloud

entry.title: Re: Dynamic runtime subreport import
entry.content:
Unfortunately, the .NET SDK does not have a direct 1:1 functional equivalent to the legacy COM-based CRAXDRT.ImportSubreport(). The standard workaround is to use the Report Application Server (RAS) SDK (available within the Visual Studio runtime) to access the SubreportController.ImportSubreport() method.

Most developers handle this by placing a "placeholder" subreport in the template and then using the RAS SDK to programmatically overwrite it with an external .rpt file at runtime. If you are scaling this in a high-availability environment, I’d recommend using contract-first testing to ensure the schema of the imported subreport doesn't break the main report’s data contracts.

entry.title: Re: LMS - Issues with course completion
entry.content:
This is a common challenge with high-volume global rollouts, @Anna_Skoncej56. The fact that users are redirected to the start often points to a "suspend_data" limit issue; SCORM 1.2 has a very small character limit (4,096 characters) for saving progress. With 200–400 MB files and multiple Synthesia videos, your package might be hitting that limit, causing the LMS to lose the bookmark and reset the attempt.

A few things to check:

  • SCORM 2004: If possible, try exporting in SCORM 2004 (3rd or 4th ed), which allows for much larger suspend_data limits.

  • Communication Seams: Large files can cause "heartbeat" timeouts between the content and the LMS. Ensure your Articulate "Send tracking data at end" settings are optimized.

  • Network & Governance: For 10,000 global users, the bandwidth required for 400 MB packages can lead to session drops. I’d recommend testing the "integration seams" via Tosca API Testing to ensure the completion signal isn't being lost in a governed middleware layer or due to latency.

entry.title: Re: Transport OBYC entries to other Clients
entry.content:
Hi Grace, since the SE16 direct transport method is restricted in S/4HANA, the recommended approach is to use the standard customizing transaction OBYC. Once you are in the transaction, go to Menu > Table View > Transport; this will prompt for a customizing request and capture the relevant T030 entries properly.

If you find that the "Transport" option is greyed out, verify that the client role in SCC4 is set to "Changes to Repository and Cross-Client Customizing Allowed". For a more automated workflow, you might consider using SAP Cloud ALM to orchestrate these transports, ensuring that your financial integration seams remain stable across QA and Production through continuous validation.

entry.title: Fullstack CAP Application with HANA Cloud (Decoupled Architecture) [Part-1]
entry.content:
Developing the CAP Application Locally with SQLite

In this blog series, I will walk you through building an Incident Management application using SAP Cloud Application Programming Model (CAP) with Node.js, connected to SAP HANA Cloud, and deployed on SAP BTP Cloud Foundry environment.

The frontend is a Fiori Elements application deployed separately to the HTML5 Application Repository — making this a fully decoupled architecture.

Here is what we will cover across this series:

— Develop the CAP application locally using SQLite (this post)
— Connect the CAP app to SAP HANA Cloud
— Deploy the CAP backend to Cloud Foundry
— Build the Fiori Elements UI using SAP Fiori tools
— Configuring Destination in BTP
— Deploy the Fiori app to the HTML5 Application Repository

Before deploying anything to the cloud, it is always a good practice to build and test your CAP application locally. In this part, we will:

- Initialize the CAP project
- Define the domain model (entities)
- Create two OData services (Admin and Processor)
- Add sample data using CSV files
- Run and test the application locally using SQLite as the database

Let's get started!


PREREQUISITES

Make sure the following tools are installed and configured on your machine before proceeding:

- Node.js (v18 or higher recommended)
- @sap/cds-dk installed globally:

npm install -g @sap/cds-dk

- VS Code with the SAP CDS Language Support extension (recommended) / Business Application Studio (BAS)
- Basic familiarity with CAP and OData concepts is helpful


STEP 1 — CREATE THE CAP PROJECT

Open a terminal and run the following command to initialize a new CAP project with Node.js support:

cds init --add nodejs incident-management

This creates a new folder called "incident-management" with the standard CAP project structure.

Navigate into the project folder:

cd incident-management

Install the required Node.js dependencies:

npm install

 

After installation, your project structure will look like this:

incident-management/
├── db/ → Domain models (entities, types)
├── srv/ → Service definitions (OData services)
├── app/ → UI applications (used in later parts)
├── package.json
└── .cdsrc.json


STEP 2 — ADD THE DOMAIN MODEL

The domain model defines all the data entities for our application.
Inside the "db" folder, create a new file named: schema.cds

Paste the following code into schema.cds:

using {
  cuid,
  managed,
  sap.common.CodeList
} from '@sap/cds/common';

namespace sap.capire.incidents;

/**
* Incidents created by Customers.
*/
entity Incidents : cuid, managed {
  customer     : Association to Customers;
  title        : String @title: 'Title';
  urgency      : Association to Urgency default 'M';
  status       : Association to Status default 'N';
  conversation : Composition of many {
                   key ID        : UUID;
                       timestamp : type of managed : createdAt;
                       author    : type of managed : createdBy;
                       message   : String;
                 };
}

/**
* Customers entitled to create support Incidents.
*/
entity Customers : managed {
  key ID           : String;
      firstName    : String;
      lastName     : String;
      name         : String = trim(firstName || ' ' || lastName);
      email        : EMailAddress;
      phone        : PhoneNumber;
      incidents    : Association to many Incidents
                       on incidents.customer = $self;
      creditCardNo : String(16) @assert.format: '^[1-9]\d{15}$';
      addresses    : Composition of many Addresses
                       on addresses.customer = $self;
}

entity Addresses : cuid, managed {
  customer      : Association to Customers;
  city          : String;
  postCode      : String;
  streetAddress : String;
}

entity Status : CodeList {
  key code        : String enum {
        new = 'N';
        assigned = 'A';
        in_process = 'I';
        on_hold = 'H';
        resolved = 'R';
        closed = 'C';
      };
      criticality : Integer;
}

entity Urgency : CodeList {
  key code        : String enum {
        high = 'H';
        medium = 'M';
        low = 'L';
      };
      criticality : Integer;
}

type EMailAddress : String;
type PhoneNumber  : String;

The model uses the namespace: sap.capire.incidents

It imports three built-in CDS types from @sap/cds/common:

- cuid → automatically generates a UUID as the primary key.
- managed → automatically tracks createdAt, createdBy, modifiedAt, modifiedBy.
- CodeList → a base type for value-list entities like Status and Urgency.

Here is a description of each entity defined in the schema:

INCIDENTS
The core entity of the application. Each incident is raised by a Customer and has a title, an urgency level (default: Medium), and a status (default: New). It also contains an inline Composition of conversations — meaning each incident can have multiple conversation/comment entries stored together with it.

CUSTOMERS
Represents the customers who raise incidents. Stores their first name, last name, email, phone, and credit card number. The "name" field is a computed field that automatically combines firstName and lastName. Each customer can have multiple Addresses and multiple Incidents linked to them.

ADDRESSES
Stores the physical address of a customer, including city, postal code, and street address. It is a composition child of Customers.

STATUS
A code list entity representing the lifecycle stages of an incident:
N → New
A → Assigned
I → In Process
H → On Hold
R → Resolved
C → Closed
Each status has a criticality value used for UI color coding in Fiori.

URGENCY
A code list entity representing the priority level of an incident:
H → High
M → Medium
L → Low
Each urgency level also carries a criticality value for UI color coding.


STEP 3 — CREATE THE SERVICES

CAP uses service definitions to expose your entities as OData endpoints.
We will create two separate service files inside the "srv" folder.

a) incidents_admin.cds

This service is used by administrators to manage customers and incidents. It exposes the Customers entity (with OData Draft enabled for edit flows) and the Incidents entity.

The service is published at the path: /odata/v4/incidents-admin

using {sap.capire.incidents as my} from '../db/schema';


/**
 * Service used by administrators to manage customers and incidents.
 */

 : 'incidents-admin'
service AdminService {
    .draft.enabled
    entity Customers as projection on my.Customers;
    entity Incidents as projection on my.Incidents;
}


b) incidents_processor.cds

This service is used by support personnel (incident processors). It exposes the Incidents entity (with OData Draft enabled) as the main working entity. The Customers entity is auto-exposed as a read-only entity — meaning processors can view customer data when working on an incident, but cannot modify it.

The service is published at the path: /odata/v4/incidents-processor

using {sap.capire.incidents as my} from '../db/schema';

/**
 * Service used by support personell, i.e. the incidents' 'processors'.
 */


@path: 'incidents-processor'
service ProcessorService  {
    .draft.enabled
    entity Incidents as projection on my.Incidents;

    // 
    // entity Customers as projection on my.Customers;

    annotate my.Customers with .autoexpose; // Exposes Customers entity as read only entity
}


> WHY TWO SERVICES?
Separating admin and processor services is a best practice in CAP. It lets you assign different authorization roles to each service and expose only what each user role actually needs. This follows the  principle of least privilege and makes it easier to apply security restrictions later (covered in a future part of this series).


STEP 4 — GENERATE CSV TEMPLATES AND ADD INITIAL DATA

CAP provides a built-in command to auto-generate CSV template files based on your domain model.

Run:

cds add data

This creates empty CSV files inside a newly created "db/data" folder.

The file names follow the pattern: <namespace>-<EntityName>.csv
e.g., sap.capire.incidents-Customers.csv

CAP automatically picks up these files and loads them as seed/initial data every time the application starts in development mode.

Now add the following sample data to each file:

a) db/data/sap.capire.incidents-Addresses.csv
Contains address records for each customer (city, postal code, street).

ID,customer_ID,city,postCode,streetAddress
17e00347-dc7e-4ca9-9c5d-06ccef69f064,1004155,Rome,00164,Piazza Adriana
d8e797d9-6507-4aaa-b43f-5d2301df5135,1004161,Munich,80809,Olympia Park
ff13d2fa-e00f-4ee5-951c-3303f490777b,1004100,Walldorf,69190,Dietmar-Hopp-Allee


b) db/data/sap.capire.incidents-Customers.csv
Contains three sample customers with ID, name, email, and phone.

ID,firstName,lastName,email,phone
1004155,Daniel,Watts,daniel.watts@demo.com,+39-555-123
1004161,Stormy,Weathers,stormy.weathers@demo.com,+49-020-022
1004100,Sunny,Sunshine,sunny.sunshine@demo.com,+49-555-789


c) db/data/sap.capire.incidents-Incidents.conversation.csv
Contains conversation/comment entries linked to incidents via the
"up__ID" foreign key (CAP convention for composition parent).

ID,up__ID,timestamp,author,message
2b23bb4b-4ac7-4a24-ac02-aa10cabd842c,3b23bb4b-4ac7-4a24-ac02-aa10cabd842c,1995-12-17T03:24:00Z,Harry John,Can you please check if battery connections are fine?
2b23bb4b-4ac7-4a24-ac02-aa10cabd843c,3a4ede72-244a-4f5f-8efa-b17e032d01ee,1995-12-18T04:24:00Z,Emily Elizabeth,Can you please check if there are any loose connections?
9583f982-d7df-4aad-ab26-301d4a157cd7,3583f982-d7df-4aad-ab26-301d4a157cd7,2022-09-04T12:00:00Z,Sunny Sunshine,Please check why the solar panel is broken.
9583f982-d7df-4aad-ab26-301d4a158cd7,3ccf474c-3881-44b7-99fb-59a2a4668418,2022-09-04T13:00:00Z,Bradley Flowers,What exactly is wrong?


d) db/data/sap.capire.incidents-Incidents.csv
Contains four sample incidents with title, urgency code, status code, and a reference to the customer who raised them.

ID,customer_ID,title,urgency_code,status_code
3b23bb4b-4ac7-4a24-ac02-aa10cabd842c,1004155,Inverter not functional,H,C
3a4ede72-244a-4f5f-8efa-b17e032d01ee,1004161,No current on a sunny day,H,N
3ccf474c-3881-44b7-99fb-59a2a4668418,1004161,Strange noise when switching off Inverter,M,N
3583f982-d7df-4aad-ab26-301d4a157cd7,1004100,Solar panel broken,H,I


e) db/data/sap.capire.incidents-Status.csv
Contains the status code list: N, A, I, H, R, C with descriptions and criticality values.

code,descr,criticality
N,New,0
A,Assigned,0
I,In Process,2
H,On Hold,0
R,Resolved,3
C,Closed,1


f) db/data/sap.capire.incidents-Urgency.csv
Contains the urgency code list: H, M, L with descriptions and criticality values.

code,descr,criticality
H,High,1
M,Medium,2
L,Low,3


STEP 5 — RUN THE PROJECT WITH SQLITE

First, deploy the schema to a local SQLite database file:

cds deploy --to sqlite

Then start the development server with live-reload:

cds watch

Once the server starts, open your browser and go to: http://localhost:4004 in your browser.

You should see the CDS welcome page listing all available OData service endpoints:

 

The two services and their exposed entities will be listed:

/odata/v4/incidents-admin
→ Customers (with Fiori preview)
→ Incidents (with Fiori preview)

/odata/v4/incidents-processor
→ Incidents (with Fiori preview)
→ Customers (with Fiori preview — read-only)

You can click the "Fiori preview" link next to any entity to open an auto-generated Fiori Elements UI — useful for quick testing without building a full UI yet.

You can also directly access the raw OData feed in the browser.

For example: http://localhost:4004/odata/v4/incidents-processor/Incidents

This will return the incident records from your CSV seed data in JSON format, confirming that the data was loaded correctly.

 


SUMMARY

In Part 1, we successfully:

✔ Created a CAP Node.js project using cds init
✔ Defined the domain model with Incidents, Customers, Addresses, Conversations, Status, and Urgency entities
✔ Created two OData services — AdminService and ProcessorService —with appropriate entity exposure for each user role
✔ Added initial seed data using CSV files
✔ Ran and validated the application locally using SQLite

The application is fully functional locally. In Part 2 , we will replace SQLite with SAP HANA Cloud and prepare the project for cloud deployment.

entry.title: Re: Understanding Tax Code Determination in Purchase Orders (SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud)
entry.content:
This is a very clear breakdown of the determination hierarchy, @Kerisha123! The visual flow emphasizing TTX1 as the priority over info records is a great reminder for both Finance and Procurement teams.

I especially like the proactive approach of using the BAdI MM_PUR_S4_PO_MODIFY_ITEM to enforce data consistency. In complex global environments—like those managing smart port logistics or multi-stakeholder infrastructure—preventing an empty tax code at the point of entry is far more efficient than troubleshooting downstream financial errors.

One quick thought: for those utilizing automated middleware or local AI tools to trigger POs, ensure your API contracts are validated through tools like Tricentis Tosca to confirm that these tax codes are populating correctly before they hit the ledger.

entry.title: Re: Authentication and Identity Management in SAP Signavio: Where We Are and What's Next
entry.content:
This harmonization is a major step forward for enterprise governance, @Jelisaveta_Nikolic! Transitioning to SAP Cloud Identity Services (SCI) not only solves the "spreadsheet fatigue" for admins but also establishes the necessary security baseline—like native MFA and SCIM v2—that modern audits demand.

From an implementation standpoint, the move toward a unified identity foundation is what truly enables a "Clean Core" strategy. By centralizing the user lifecycle, we can ensure that "integration seams" between SAP Signavio and the rest of the SAP ecosystem remain secure and transparent.

I’m particularly looking forward to the SCIM v2 support for directory synchronization with Microsoft Entra ID. For those of us leveraging automated testing tools like Tricentis Tosca, having a single source of truth for identity makes validating cross-application workflows significantly more reliable.

entry.title: Pessimistic Concurrency Control (Locking) using Unmanaged Scenario in RAP
entry.content:
Introduction: 

SAP RESTful Application Programming Model (RAP), locking has a major impact because if multiple users can update the same record simultaneously, it leads to data inconsistencies or overwrites. To work on this concurrency control, we have two different approaches-  

  1. Pessimistic concurrency control (locking). 
  2. Optimistic concurrency control (Etag or Odata Etag). 

In a RAP unmanaged scenario, pessimistic concurrency control (locking) is particularly useful because it assumes conflicts are likely and prevents them upfront by locking the record as soon as a user starts editing (like delete, update or modify operations) it. In an unmanaged scenario, the RAP framework does not automatically handle locking. Developers are responsible for implementing the entire locking mechanism manually. 

Pessimistic Concurrency Control (Locking): In RAP business objects, enqueue locks are used for pessimistic concurrency control. Exclusive locking happens on data. 

  • Exclusive Locking: Before an operation (like an UPDATE or DELETE) is executed, the RAP framework acquires an exclusive lock on the specific data instance in a global lock table. 
  • Every time a lock is requested, the system checks the lock table. If another user already holds a lock on that specific record, the new request is rejected immediately by throwing an error message. 
  •  The lock is released in the scenarios like, 
  1. ABAP session gets terminated. 
  2.  LUW gets terminated by calling commit or Rollback. 
  3.  Draft scenario is discarded or expired.

Scenario: 

In this application, we have a Sales (Header) table (ZDB_SALES_HDR) and Sales (Item) table (ZDB_SALES_ITEM) linked through so_id. Need to set the actual database lock by explicitly calling the standard Enqueue Object inside the FOR LOCK method. Users should also be able to save incomplete data as draft and complete it later. To handle custom logic and manual persistence, an unmanaged RAP approach is used. 

1. Database tables:

@EndUserText.label : 'Header sales table' 
@AbapCatalog.enhancement.category : #NOT_EXTENSIBLE 
@AbapCatalog.tableCategory : #TRANSPARENT 
@AbapCatalog.deliveryClass : #A 
@AbapCatalog.dataMaintenance : #RESTRICTED 
define table zdb_sales_hdr { 

key client  : abap.clnt not null; 
key so_id   : abap.char(10) not null; 
customer_id : abap.char(10); 
order_date  : sydatum; 
@Semantics.amount.currencyCode : 'zdb_sales_hdr.currency' 
total_amount: abap.curr(10,2); 
currency    : abap.cuky; 
@Semantics.amount.currencyCode : ' zdb_sales_hdr.currency' 
discount_pct: abap.curr(10,2); 
status      : zde_or_status; 
created_by  : syuname; 
created_on  : timestampl; 
approved_by : syuname; 
approved_on : sydatum; 
}

 

@EndUserText.label : 'Item sales table' 
@AbapCatalog.enhancement.category : #NOT_EXTENSIBLE 
@AbapCatalog.tableCategory : #TRANSPARENT 
@AbapCatalog.deliveryClass : #A 
@AbapCatalog.dataMaintenance : #RESTRICTED 

define table zdb_sales_item { 
key client  : abap.clnt not null; 
key so_id   : abap.char(10) not null; 
item_no     : abap.char(10); 
material_id : abap.char(10); 
@Semantics.quantity.unitOfMeasure : ' zdb_sales_item.uom' 
quantity    : abap.quan(5,2); 
uom         : meins; 
@Semantics.amount.currencyCode : ' zdb_sales_item.currency' 
price       : abap.curr(10,2); 
currency    : abap.cuky; 
plant       : werks_d; 
}

2. Created draft tables for both Sales (Header) and Sales (Item) entities to support draft functionality. These tables store intermediate data, allowing users to save incomplete records and activate them later.

@EndUserText.label : 'Draft table for entity ZI_HDR_SALES'
@AbapCatalog.enhancement.category : #EXTENSIBLE_ANY
@AbapCatalog.tableCategory : #TRANSPARENT
@AbapCatalog.deliveryClass : #A
@AbapCatalog.dataMaintenance : #RESTRICTED
define table zdb_header_draft {

  key mandt     : mandt not null;
  key soid      : abap.char(10) not null;
  key draftuuid : sdraft_uuid not null;
  customerid    : abap.char(10);
  orderdate     : sydatum;
  @Semantics.amount.currencyCode : 'zdb_header_draft.currency'
  totalamount   : abap.curr(10,2);
  currency      : abap.cuky;
  @Semantics.amount.currencyCode : 'zdb_header_draft.currency'
  discountpct   : abap.curr(10,2);
  status        : abap.char(10);
  createdby     : syuname;
  createdon     : timestampl;
  approvedby    : syuname;
  approvedon    : sydatum;
  "%admin"      : include sych_bdl_draft_admin_inc;
}

 

@EndUserText.label : 'Draft table for entity ZI_ITEM_SALES'
@AbapCatalog.enhancement.category : #EXTENSIBLE_ANY
@AbapCatalog.tableCategory : #TRANSPARENT
@AbapCatalog.deliveryClass : #A
@AbapCatalog.dataMaintenance : #RESTRICTED
define table zdb_item_draft {

  key mandt       : mandt not null;
  key soid        : abap.char(10) not null;
  key draftuuid   : sdraft_uuid not null;
  parentdraftuuid : sdraft_uuid;
  itemno          : abap.char(10);
  materialid      : abap.char(10);
  @Semantics.quantity.unitOfMeasure : 'zdb_item_draft.uom'
  quantity        : abap.quan(5,2);
  uom             : meins;
  @Semantics.amount.currencyCode : 'zdb_item_draft.currency'
  price           : abap.curr(10,2);
  currency        : abap.cuky;
  plant           : werks_d;
  "%admin"        : include sych_bdl_draft_admin_inc;
}

3. Created an Interface view on top of these two database table. Defined a proper association between Sales (Header) and Sales (Item) using so_id to establish the relationship. 

@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #CHECK 
@EndUserText.label: 'Interface view for sales header table' 
@Metadata.ignorePropagatedAnnotations: true 

define root view entity ZI_HDR_SALES  
as select from zdb_sales_hdr 
composition [1..*] of ZI_ITEM_SALES as _salesitem 
{ 
key so_id    as SoId, 
customer_id  as CustomerId, 
order_date   as OrderDate, 
@Semantics.amount.currencyCode: 'Currency' 
total_amount as TotalAmount, 
currency     as Currency, 
@Semantics.amount.currencyCode: 'Currency' 
discount_pct as DiscountPct, 
status       as Status, 
created_by   as CreatedBy, 
created_on   as CreatedOn, 
approved_by  as ApprovedBy, 
approved_on  as ApprovedOn, 
_salesitem 
}

 

@AbapCatalog.viewEnhancementCategory: [#NONE] 
@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED 
@EndUserText.label: 'Interface view for sales item table' 
@Metadata.ignorePropagatedAnnotations: true 
@ObjectModel.usageType:{ 
serviceQuality: #X, 
sizeCategory: #S, 
dataClass: #MIXED 
} 
define view entity ZI_ITEM_SALES  
as select from zdb_sales_item 
association to parent ZI_HDR_SALES as _header 
on $projection.SoId = _header.SoId 
{ 
key so_id   as SoId, 
item_no     as ItemNo, 
material_id as MaterialId, 
@Semantics.quantity.unitOfMeasure: 'Uom' 
quantity    as Quantity, 
uom         as Uom, 
@Semantics.amount.currencyCode: 'Currency' 
price       as Price, 
currency    as Currency, 
plant       as Plant, 
_header 
}

4. Created a consumption CDS view on top of the interface views to expose the data for UI usage. Instead of using metadata extensions, all required UI annotations were directly maintained within the consumption view for simplicity. 

@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #CHECK 
@EndUserText.label: 'Projection view for sales header table' 
@Metadata.ignorePropagatedAnnotations: true 
@Metadata.allowExtensions: true 

define root view entity ZC_HDR_SALES  
provider contract transactional_query 
as projection on ZI_HDR_SALES 
{ 
@UI.facet: [ 
{ position: 10, label: 'Sales Header', purpose: #STANDARD, type: #IDENTIFICATION_REFERENCE }, 
{ position: 20, label: 'Sales Item', purpose: #STANDARD, type: #LINEITEM_REFERENCE, targetElement: '_salesitem' } 
] 

@UI: { 
lineItem: [{ position: 10, label: 'Sales Id' }], 
identification: [{ position: 10, label: 'Sales Id' }] 
} 
SoId; 

@UI: { 
lineItem: [{ position: 20, label: 'Customer Id' }], 
identification: [{ position: 20, label: 'Customer Id' }] 
} 
CustomerId; 

@UI: { 
lineItem: [{ position: 30, label: 'Order date' }], 
identification: [{ position: 30, label: 'Order date' }] 
} 
OrderDate; 

@UI: { 
lineItem: [{ position: 40, label: 'Total amount' }], 
identification: [{ position: 40, label: 'Total Amount' }] 
} 
TotalAmount; 

@UI: { 
lineItem: [{ position: 50, label: 'Currency' }], 
identification: [{ position: 50, label: 'Currency' }] 
} 
Currency; 

@UI: { 
lineItem: [{ position: 60 }], 
identification: [{ position: 60, label: 'Discount price' }] 
} 

@UI.hidden: true 
DiscountPct; 

@UI: { 
lineItem: [{ position: 70, label: 'Order Status' }], 
identification: [{ position: 70, label: 'Status' }] 
} 

@Consumption.valueHelpDefinition: [{  
entity:{  
name : 'ZI_NK_STATUS_VH', 
element: 'Status' 
} 
}] 
Status; 

@UI: { 
lineItem: [{ position: 80, label: 'Created by' }], 
identification: [{ position: 80, label: 'Created by' }] 
} 
CreatedBy; 

@UI: { 
lineItem: [{ position: 90, label: 'Created on' }], 
identification: [{ position: 90, label: 'Created on' }] 
} 
CreatedOn; 

@UI: { 
lineItem: [{ position: 100 }], 
identification: [{ position: 100, label: 'Approved by' }] 
} 

@UI.hidden: true 
ApprovedBy; 

@UI: { 
lineItem: [{ position: 110 }], 
identification: [{ position: 110, label: 'Approved on' }] 
} 

@UI.hidden: true 
ApprovedOn; 
_salesitem : redirected to composition child ZC_ITEM_SALES 
}

 

@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED 
@EndUserText.label: 'Projection view for sales itemtable' 
@Metadata.ignorePropagatedAnnotations: true 
@Metadata.allowExtensions: true 

define view entity ZC_ITEM_SALES  
as projection on ZI_ITEM_SALES 
{ 
@UI.facet: [{ position: 10, label: 'Sales Item', purpose: #STANDARD, type: #IDENTIFICATION_REFERENCE }] 

@UI:{ 
lineItem: [{ position: 10, label: 'Sales Id' }], 
identification: [{ position: 10, label: 'Sales Id' }] 
} 
SoId; 

@UI:{ 
lineItem: [{ position: 20, label: 'Item no'}], 
identification: [{ position: 20, label: 'Item no' }] 
} 
ItemNo; 

@UI:{ 
lineItem: [{ position: 30, label: 'Material Id' }], 
identification: [{ position: 30, label: 'Material id' }] 
} 
MaterialId; 

@UI:{ 
lineItem: [{ position: 40, label: 'Quantity' }], 
identification: [{ position: 40, label: 'Quantity' }] 
} 
Quantity; 

@UI:{ 
lineItem: [{ position: 50, label: 'Unit' }], 
identification: [{ position: 50, label: 'Unit of measure' }] 
} 

@UI.hidden: true 
Uom; 

@UI:{ 
lineItem: [{ position: 60, label: 'Price'}], 
identification: [{ position: 60, label: 'Price' }] 
} 
Price; 

@UI:{ 
lineItem: [{ position: 70 }], 
identification: [{ position: 70, label: 'Currency' }] 
} 
Currency; 

@UI:{ 
lineItem: [{ position: 80, label: 'Plant'}], 
identification: [{ position: 80, label: 'Plant' }] 
} 
Plant; 
_header : redirected to parent ZC_HDR_SALES 
}

5. Created behavior definitions on top of the Sales (Header) interface view. Defined the required operations and enabled features like draft handling, associations, specified lock master (for the root entity) and lock dependent (for child entity) to tell the framework that locking is required as per our requirements. 

Specify lock master (for the root entity). 

define behavior for ZI_HDR_SALES //alias <alias_name> 
draft table zdb_header_draft 
late numbering 
lock master 
total etag CreatedBy 
authorization master ( instance ) 
{ 
create; 
update ( features : instance ); 
delete ( features : instance ); 
field ( readonly ) SoId; 
}

Specify lock dependent (for child entity). 

define behavior for ZI_ITEM_SALES //alias <alias_name> 
draft table zdb_item_draft 
late numbering 
lock dependent by _header 
authorization dependent by _header 
{ 
update; 
delete; 
field ( readonly ) SoId; 
association _header { with draft; } 
}

5. Created a lock object by providing Sales (Header) as Primary table. 

Selected lock mode as write lock. 

Activated, Generated Enqueue and Dequeue Objects. 

6. Implemented the required business logic in the behavior pool class using a helper class. This includes handling calculations, and manual data persistence for both Sales entities. 

Inside the FOR LOCK method in the behavior pool class, must explicitly call the created lock object( ‘EZLOCK_SALES' ) to set the actual database lock. 

METHOD lock. 
TRY. 
DATA(lock) = cl_abap_lock_object_factory=>get_instance( iv_name = 'EZLOCK_SALES' ). 
CATCH cx_abap_lock_failure INTO DATA(exception). 
RAISE SHORTDUMP exception. 
ENDTRY. 

LOOP AT keys ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL(<fs_data>). 
TRY. 
lock->enqueue( 
it_parameter = VALUE #( ( name = 'Sales Id' value = REF #( <fs_data>-SoId ) ) ) 
). 
CATCH cx_abap_foreign_lock INTO DATA(foreign_lock). 

APPEND VALUE #( 
SoId = keys[ 1 ]-SoId 
%msg = new_message_with_text( 
severity = if_abap_behv_message=>severity-error 
text = 'Record is locked by ' && foreign_lock->user_name 
) 
) TO reported-zi_hdr_sales. 

APPEND VALUE #( 
SoId = keys[ 1 ]-SoId 
) TO failed-zi_hdr_sales. 
CATCH cx_abap_lock_failure INTO exception. 
RAISE SHORTDUMP exception. 
ENDTRY. 
ENDLOOP. 
ENDMETHOD.

Output: 

If both the users are accessing the same application then, 

  • First user opens the record in edit mode.  
  • Trigger the operation that calls the lock method (e.g., Edit action).  
  • If the second user try to edit the same record.  

System should prevent editing by throwing Error message like: “Record is locked by another user”.  

Conclusion:

Pessimistic concurrency control (locking) in an unmanaged RAP scenario is fundamental to safeguarding business-critical data by preventing concurrent modifications at the source. It shifts control entirely to the developer, making correct lock design essential for ensuring consistency, reliability, and transactional integrity. While it may introduce contention and affect performance if misused, a well-implemented locking strategy delivers predictable system behavior and eliminates costly data conflicts. Ultimately, it is a key pillar for building robust, enterprise-grade RAP applications where data accuracy cannot be compromised.

entry.title: Re: Where the logic is written to populate KNVV table whe...
entry.content:
In S/4HANA (and ECC), while XK02 is the transaction, the actual update logic is encapsulated within the function module SD_CUSTOMER_MAINTAIN_ALL or the newer VMD_EI_API class methods, which handle the synchronization of the Customer/Vendor integration (CVI) if your vendor is also a customer.

Specifically, the shipping condition field (VSBED) is mapped during the PAI (Process After Input) of the vendor master screens, where the system triggers the VMD_DATA_SAVE function. If you are looking to debug or enhance this, check the BAdI CVI_CUSTOM_MAPPER or the enhancement spots in program SAPMF02K. For those of us prioritizing a "Clean Core," I'd recommend using SAP Cloud ALM to orchestrate the testing of these integration points to ensure your manual changes in XK02 don't disrupt downstream shipping logic.

entry.title: ABAP RAP: Multiple Action Groups in Fiori Elements App
entry.content:
Hi all,

I’m working with RAP Treeview, and I’m trying to use multiple action groups in @ui.lineItem.

My setup includes multiple #FOR_ACTION_GROUP entries, each with its own actionGroupId, and corresponding actions assigned to each group.

Example (simplified):

@ui.lineItem: [
{ type: #FOR_ACTION_GROUP, label: 'Group A', actionGroupId: 'A', position: 10 },
{ type: #FOR_ACTION_GROUP, label: 'Group B', actionGroupId: 'B', position: 20 },

{ type: #FOR_ACTION, dataAction: 'action1', actionGroupId: 'A', position: 30 },
{ type: #FOR_ACTION, dataAction: 'action2', actionGroupId: 'B', position: 40 }
]

Issue:

Only one action group is rendered in the UI. The others are ignored.
After activation, the following warning is displayed:

Action group is not supported [RAP Designtime], Message ID SADL_GW_DT_CDS(093)

Behavior definitions and actions are working correctly, including multi-select and invocationGrouping: #CHANGE_SET.

Questions:

Can I include multiple action groups in Fiori Elements?
If yes., how? If no, what is the reason for this?

Best,
Romanos

entry.title: SAP Concur Integration: Follow SAP Activate Roadmap for Your Cloud Transformation
entry.content:
For customers upgrading or migrating their SAP Concur integration (SAP ICS)—whether from SAP ECC to SAP S/4HANA or from SAP S/4HANA Private Cloud (RISE) to Public Cloud (GROW)—following the SAP Activate Roadmap. is strongly recommended.

SAP Activate provides standardized, solution‑specific guidance for planning and executing ERP transformations, including integration‑relevant activities and quality gates. The SAP Activate Roadmap Viewer is available via SAP for Me and helps align SAP Concur Integration decisions with the overall S/4HANA migration approach. 

As part of SAP Activate, customers should rely on Fit‑to‑Standard and integration workshops to:

  • Validate existing on-premise‑based integration patterns
  • Align with supported SAP ICS capabilities in S/4HANA
  • Understand differences between Private Cloud and Public Cloud integration models


Official Documentation and SAP Notes

During both planning and execution, we strongly recommend relying on official SAP documentation to ensure a consistent, supported approach.
SAP Activate Roadmap Viewer 

The SAP Activate Roadmap provides structured, solution‑specific guidance, including relevant activities and quality gates. Recommended roadmaps include:

  • SAP Activate for SAP Concur
  • SAP Activate for SAP Cloud ERP Private Edition
  • SAP Activate for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition

SAP Integration with Concur Solutions for S/4HANA Cloud
  • SAP Help Portal Documentation for SAP Integration with Concur Solutions 

Key Documentation and SAP Notes for SAP Concur Integration

    • 3140635 – Release Information: SAP S/4HANA Cloud and the Concur Integration
    • 2922806 – FAQ: General Information about the Concur Integration
    • 2924469 – FAQ: Concur Integration (S/4HANA Cloud)
    • 3483312 – SAP Concur Integration: Transitioning from SAP ECC or SAP S/4HANA
                             to SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud

Need Help?

If you’re looking for guidance on SAP Concur integration (SAP ICS) as part of your cloud transformation journey, our integration team is here to support you: sapconcurintegration@sap.com

entry.title: Re: Print via output control indicator - Goods movement

entry.title: Re: Print via output control indicator - Goods movement
entry.content:
Hello @Moon3

Thank you for the clarification.

Since you are using MIGO, the default value of Print via Output Control is not controlled only by Output Parameter Determination. OPD controls whether the output can be determined once the transaction data matches the rule, but the default selection of the checkbox in MIGO is linked to the MIGO print indicator behaviour and the user parameter NDR.

Compare the user master parameter in DEV and TEST.  Make sure in your Parameters tab, you maintain parameter ID NDR with value X. Save, log off and log on again. Open MIGO and check whether Print via Output Control is manually selected.

Regarding transport configuration, I would mainly compare these items between DEV and TEST:

  • Output Parameter Determination – Goods Movement – Output Type, Receiver, Channel, Printer Settings, Form Template
  • Print Active, Print Item, Print Version, Movement Type, whether all OPD rules are activated in your Test environment and whether the relevant software collection or configuration transport was successfully imported into your TEST environment.

However, if all I mentioned above are truly identical, then it would be an OPD transport issue. Hence why you should first check the NDR user parameter behaviour in your TEST environment.

If the checkbox is now automatically selected, then the root cause was the missing or inconsistent user parameter in you TEST environment. You can compare the full OPD setup and raise a case with SAP on this matter. You may reference KBAs 3079393 and 3741385 while doing that.

 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Accrual Management in SAP S4 Public Cloud
entry.content:
Hi All,

We are implementing SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud (PSA) for a professional services firm. As part of the month‑end close, the client must post accruals to reflect project costs incurred but not yet received (Supplier Invoices not loaded to the project)—examples include travel and accommodation for internal and external staff assigned to projects. A core requirement is that these accruals should reverse automatically once the actual costs are posted. Our proposed approach is to use Accrual Management (the accrual engine) for PO‑based accruals, and to use the “Post General Journal Entries” app with the standard template (which supports a planned reversal date) for manual accruals. In addition to this, would accruals drive revenue recognition as well similar to actual cost. We welcome advice from the community on any additional SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud functionality or alternative approaches to meet this requirement.

Appreciate your comment and feedback.

Thank you

entry.title: SAP Community Voice: Updates based on your Feedback, SAP experts Blog Posts and SAP Sapphire 2026
entry.content:
Explore our latest technical blog posts covering SAP Fiori annotations, People Intelligence in SAP Business Data Cloud, and automation tips for SAP Build. Plus find out more details about a new graphical user interface now available directly in the SAP Business Technology Platform cockpit. 

We also want to highlight several platform enhancements that are now live. These changes were developed based on the feedback and discussions happening in our forums and comments; our team has been working to address these needs and create a more intuitive experience. 

Let´s dive in!

Featured Blog Posts 

SAP Fiori Elements: Essentials for XML Annotations 

Working with SAPUI5 Fiori Elements often means relying on annotation examples from the UI5 Demo Kit, but simply copying those snippets can quickly lead to frustration when they don't work as expected. SAP Champion Mio Yasutake explains in her latest blog post exactly why that happens, focusing on two key aspects: correctly defining the annotation target and including the proper OData vocabulary reference. Using clear CAP and RAP examples, Mio shows how to extract the right values from metadata.xml and adapt documentation samples step by step into a working annotation.xml. It's a very practical guide that helps developers move from trial-and-error to a more structured and confident approach when working with annotations. 

Read Mio Yasutake blog post here  

People Intelligence in SAP Business Data Cloud 

Traditionally, consultants relied on SAP SuccessFactors OData APIs to extract HR data into tools like SAP Datasphere and SAP Analytics Cloud, which required significant modeling effort and could lead to performance challenges as data volumes increased. In this blog post, SAP expert Zili Zhou demonstrates how People Intelligence, delivered as part of SAP Business Data Cloud, replaces this approach with a native, pre-built analytical framework that hides API complexity, improves data consistency, and provides a near real-time, end-to-end view of the workforce. The post walks through the full configuration process across BDC Cockpit, SuccessFactors, Datasphere, and SAC, including activating data packages, installing intelligent applications, setting up data access control, and validating reports. We highly recommend checking the full post, because in the end you will also find the glossary with valuable links 

Read Zili Zhou blog post here  

Tips on using SAP Build Process Automation 

Instead of triggering integrations manually, this blog post by SAP Community member Ajay Wandre shows a practical way to automate the process by combining SAP Build Process Automation with SAP Integration Suite. Using a step-by-step approach, Ajay demonstrates how to trigger a CPI iFlow through an automation job-starting with creating and activating an iFlow, configuring destinations in SAP Business Technology Platform, and building actions and automation artifacts inside SAP Build. The post walks through the full setup, including deployment, Desktop Agent configuration, and scheduling triggers, ultimately showing how CPI iFlows can be executed automatically and monitored with response payloads-making it a useful example of how to streamline integration processes using automation. 

Read Ajay Wandre blog post here 

First graphical user interface directly from the BTP Cockpit 

Following the initial release of the Application Vulnerability Report API, this blog by SAP expert Fabian Richter introduces a major enhancement: a new graphical user interface now available directly in the SAP Business Technology Platform cockpit. Designed to complement the existing API-based approach, the UI provides a more intuitive and accessible way for developers, DevOps engineers, and security teams to understand and act on open-source vulnerabilities in Cloud Foundry applications. With features like a centralized findings dashboard, severity breakdown by subaccount, and detailed views including CVSS scores, CVEs, and remediation guidance, the tool enables faster prioritization and resolution of security issues. By combining SAP's existing scanning capabilities with a visual layer, this update makes vulnerability management more actionable across the entire BTP landscape. Make sure to read the full content and to try out the UI and give your feedback in the comments. The team would appreciate hearing if the dashboard gave you the overview you needed and what you would like to see next.  

Read Fabian Richter blog post here 

Community News 

SAP Community Enhancements 

Over the past few months, our team has been diving deep into your comments and listening closely to the discussions happening across our community. We have heard your feedback loud and clear: you wanted a platform that feels more intuitive, keeps content fresh, and prioritizes the needs of the people using it every day. We’re thrilled to roll out these updates, shaped directly by your feedback, and particularly excited to debut the 60-day 'Recent Activity' filter!  

Find more details about each one of these new enhancements here: 

Improved Content Discovery in Recent Activity Feeds 

Recent Activity feeds across the main interaction styles have been refined to highlight more relevant and timely content: 

  • Most viewed, most commented, and most liked filters now focus on recently created content (last 60 days), instead of pulling very old posts. 
  • This ensures that highly engaged recent discussions are easier to find. 

Easier Joining and Following (Even When Not Logged In) 

New and anonymous visitors are now better guided to engage with the community: 

  • Join Group buttons are now visible even when you are not logged in 
    If you click “Join” while logged out, you’ll be prompted to log in or register, and the join action will complete automatically after login. 
  • Subscribe buttons are now visible when logged out 
    On tag pages, users can immediately see the option to follow a topic, encouraging discovery and return visits.  

A Cleaner, More Relevant Events Calendar 

We’ve improved how events appear in the main Community Events Calendar: 

  • Events created within Location Groups are now more selectively promoted to the main "All Location Events" community calendar 
  • The main calendar focuses on broader, community‑relevant location events 
  • All Group events remain fully visible inside their respective Groups 
  • Existing event boards remain fully visible 

Better Read / Unread Visibility 

To help you keep track of what’s new: 

  • Read / unread indicators are now available for Blog Posts in tile view, extending the functionality already available in other feeds. 

Clearer Dates and More Consistent Formatting 

Several refinements improve clarity and consistency: 

  • “Last edited” dates now respect your personal date format preferences 
    This applies especially to Knowledge Base (TKB) content. 
  • Accepted solution indicators are now consistently aligned, even when titles span multiple lines. 

Cleaner Homepage and Navigation Adjustments 

To reduce clutter and improve focus: 

  • The “users online” statistic has been removed from the homepage, simplifying the layout. 
  • Content building blocks (CCB widgets) were repositioned to better highlight important announcements now that carousels have been removed. 

Polished UI Fixes and Small Improvements 

A set of targeted refinements improves visual quality and usability: 

  • Improved alignment and spacing in notification settings 
  • Minor icon and layout adjustments for better consistency 
  • Fixes to ensure interface elements behave the same way across pages 

Read the SAP Community Enhancements blog post here for more details  

As always, please make sure to try out these updates and give us your feedback!  

Events 

SAP Sapphire 2026

SAP Sapphire 2026 is right around the corner, and we are very excited to see how our SAP community is going to be present and engaged in the sessions and meetings. In case you need more reasons to be excited, our SAP expert Anja Rosker has posted an amazing blog post about how the SAP runs SAP track centers on a straightforward question: what can be learned from how SAP operates its own business using its own technology?  

Across Orlando and Madrid, the program brings together more than 50 sessions and over 16 hours of content, led by SAP’s internal IT experts who are directly involved in running these systems. Rather than focusing on vision, the sessions provide visibility into how agents, applications, and data are applied in a real enterprise environment, covering areas such as finance, HR, procurement, customer experience, operations, and much more. Beyond the sessions, the experience extends into interactive formats like the Experience Hub, the Expert Bar, and the C-suite Experience. SAP Sapphire 2026 offers a chance to engage directly with the people and experiences behind it. Find out more in the session catalogs: Orlando and Madrid. And get a first exciting taste of what’s ahead in the short YouTube teaser videos. 

Read Anja Rosker blog post here 

SAP Community-driven events calendar: May 2026  

The first quarter of 2026 was remarkable for all the community-driven events that have happened all over the world. And we are very grateful and excited for another month filled with more opportunities for our SAP community members to get together!  

Check out now our May 2026 list: 

  • Lithuanian SAP Community Event on the 7th of May, 2026 
  • SAP Stammtisch Wien - 07.05.2026 - SAP Community 
  • SAP Stammtisch Munich (05/2026) - SAP Community 
  • Quedada SAP en Cervecería Köln - SAP Community
  • SAP Community Stammtisch Chemnitz 
  • SAP Stammtisch | SAP Community Meetup (Cologne #7) 
  • SAP Inside Track CErrado Cerrado 2026 
  • SAP Inside Track Melbourne - 20th May 2026 
  • Meetup - Sapphire Madrid 2026 - SAP Community 
  • SAP Sydney Stammtisch May '26 - SAP Community 
  • SAP Stammtisch in Basel - SAP Community 
  • SAP Essentials - workshops for everyone! - SAP Community 
  • SAP Inside Track Wroclaw 2026 - SAP Community 
  • SAP Inside Track Recife 2026 - Edição dos 10 anos ... - SAP Community 
  • SAP Stammtisch CDMX (Santa Fe) - CodeJams Latam 2... - SAP Community 
  • SAP Community CPH Meetup - 28th May 2026 - SAP Community 
  • SAP Stammtisch Monterrey - CodeJams Latam 2026 - SAP Community 
  • BTP in Rio - SAP Community

entry.title: Re: Accrual Management in SAP S4 Public Cloud
entry.content:
This is a solid approach for a Professional Services (PSA) environment, @nikesh_rathodeb6b33. Using the Accrual Engine for PO-based items and the Post General Journal Entries app for manual reversals is the standard "Clean Core" recommendation for S/4HANA Public Cloud.

To answer your question on Revenue Recognition: In the PSA scenario (specifically with Event-Based Revenue Recognition - EBRR), accruals posted via the Accrual Engine typically do not automatically drive revenue recognition like actual costs do. EBRR usually triggers based on actual cost postings to the project (WBS element). If you need these accruals to impact revenue, you may need to look into Manual Accruals for EBRR or ensure the account assignment logic in your Accrual Engine is tightly integrated with the project’s recognition key.

One alternative to consider is the Purchase Order Accruals functionality within the Accrual Engine, which can automate the "calculate-review-post" cycle more effectively than manual journal entries for recurring travel costs. Also, ensure your integration seams are validated—perhaps through Tosca API Testing—to confirm that the reversal logic doesn't create "orphan" entries in your project's financial ledger during the month-end close.

entry.title: Re: Its 2025 , yet recurring absence doesnt respect take...
entry.content:
This is a known architectural gap that continues to impact high-compliance environments, @IMZEI. Since the system processes recurring absences as discrete, independent objects, any "Take Rule" relying on a Count or Frequency check across a period fails to see the "set" as a whole.

While there isn't a direct fix on the immediate roadmap to change the validation engine's logic for recurring sets, many organizations use Integration Center or a Custom MDF Alert as a workaround. You can schedule a job to scan for "Abuse of Type" (e.g., more than X occurrences in Y weeks) and flag these for HR review after the fact.

To maintain a "Clean Core," I'd also recommend using SAP Cloud ALM to document this limitation in your business process and potentially leverage Tosca API Testing to validate that any custom middleware you build to "catch" these occurrences remains stable during SuccessFactors releases.

entry.title: CL_SALV_BS_RUNTIME_INFO returns empty data after upgrade to S/4HANA 2025
entry.content:
Description:
After upgrading S/4HANA On-Premise from 1809 to 2025, part of our custom code no longer works.

We are using CL_SALV_BS_RUNTIME_INFO to capture ALV output from a standard report. The logic worked correctly in 1809.

Simplified code:

cl_salv_bs_runtime_info=>set(
display = abap_false
metadata = abap_false
data = abap_true ).

SUBMIT fagl_line_item_browser
VIA SELECTION-SCREEN
AND RETURN.

TRY.
cl_salv_bs_runtime_info=>get_data_ref(
IMPORTING r_data = lt_data_ref ).
CATCH cx_salv_bs_sc_runtime_info.
MESSAGE 'Error when receiving data.' TYPE 'E'.
ENDTRY.

After the upgrade, lt_data_ref is always initial (empty), although the report itself displays data correctly when executed normally.

It seems that FAGL_LINE_ITEM_BROWSER might now be using ALV with IDA (HANA-based rendering) instead of classical SALV, which could explain why CL_SALV_BS_RUNTIME_INFO no longer captures the output data.

Has the behavior of CL_SALV_BS_RUNTIME_INFO changed in S/4HANA 2025, or is there a recommended alternative approach to retrieve ALV data from FAGL_LINE_ITEM_BROWSER in this case?

entry.title: Re: S/4HANA AIF enhancement for Order to Cash
entry.content:
This is a great candidate for AIF Value Mapping, @MuralitharanC. To keep your core stable, I recommend using Transaction /AIF/VMAP to create a regional mapping for the AUART field. By defining a transformation at the AIF Structure Mapping level, you can intercept the incoming web payload and switch the order type to ZXXX dynamically before the BAPI/API is called.

To ensure this only impacts creation, place the logic within the Structure Mapping specifically for the Sales Order Header of your Create interface. For high-volume O2C flows, I’d also recommend validating these "integration seams" with Tosca API Testing to confirm that the order type conversion doesn't trigger unexpected downstream errors in delivery or billing logic.

entry.title: Re: Document Info Records Lab Field
entry.content:
In the SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, the "Laboratory" field is no longer a standard header-level field in the Manage Document Info Records app.

To achieve this functionality, the recommended "Clean Core" approach is to use Characteristics and Classes (Classification System). You can define a characteristic for "Laboratory" and assign it to the Class Type 017 (Document Management). This allows your client to maintain and search for documents by lab/design office without requiring the legacy ECC field.

If they specifically need this to sync with the Material Master Lab/Office field, you might consider a BDP (Business Design Profession) logic or a simple custom field extension via the Custom Fields and Logic app to ensure your integration seams remain stable across your PLM processes.

entry.title: Re: Outboound delivery WOCR UNDE
entry.content:
This is a classic EWM determination issue, @basha_shaik7. Even though your screenshot shows that Activity Area R001 and Activity PICK are triggering correctly, the fact that the system falls back to UNDE (Undefined) means it is failing the final validation of your rule GR01.

Since you aren't using packing profiles or filters, the most likely culprit is that the Warehouse Tasks (WTs) being created do not meet the "Min" or "Max" limits defined inside the configuration of Rule GR01. If the WT quantity or weight falls outside the bounds of that specific rule, EWM rejects it and assigns the system default UNDE.

Check these three things:

  • WOCR Limits: Go to Define Warehouse Order Creation Rules and verify the "Min. Items" or "Weight/Volume" limits for GR01. If the current WT is too small, the rule won't apply.

  • Sort Sequence: Ensure that the sort sequence assigned to R001 for the PICK activity is correctly generating the queue.

  • Clean Core Validation: To maintain system stability, I recommend using SAP Cloud ALM to trace these determination failures and validating the "integration seams" with Tosca API Testing to ensure that upstream delivery data isn't missing a parameter required by your WOCR.

A few key points that make this roadmap approach particularly valuable:

  • Documentation Hierarchy: Referring to SAP Note 3483312 is vital for understanding the nuances when transitioning specifically to the Public Cloud (GROW) model.

  • Quality Gates: Following the structured quality gates in Activate helps prevent the "loss of institutional knowledge" that often occurs during rapid ERP migrations.

  • Automated Validation: For those of us using Tricentis Tosca for continuous testing, these roadmap activities provide the perfect baseline for creating automated test cases that validate the SAP ICS capabilities across every release.

Excellent compilation of resources and SAP Notes!

entry.title: Re: ABAP RAP: Multiple Action Groups in Fiori Elements App
entry.content:
This is a known limitation in the current SADL designtime, @Romanos. The warning SADL_GW_DT_CDS(093) confirms that while the CDS syntax allows multiple #FOR_ACTION_GROUP entries, the metadata engine is only equipped to process one group per collection.

To maintain a "Clean Core" and avoid UI inconsistencies, I recommend consolidating into one primary action group and placing your high-priority actions directly on the toolbar as standalone buttons. If you are scaling this for complex treeviews, you might consider validating the "integration seams" with Tosca API Testing to ensure that your single group remains robust across different service releases.

entry.title: Re: Dunning sample print was sent to customer
entry.content:
This is a stressful situation for any finance team, @FabianSCC. In SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, the issue often stems from the Output Parameter Determination (OPD) rules being set to trigger an "Email" channel automatically whenever the "Print" action is initiated, even for sample runs.

To prevent this from happening again, you should adjust your output settings to ensure sample printouts are restricted to a local print spool or a safe preview:

Corrective Steps in Output Parameter Determination (OPD)

  • Check the Dispatch Time: In the OPD transaction, look at the Dispatch Time rule for the Dunning document. If it is set to "1 (Immediately)", the system will trigger the email as soon as the print job is scheduled. Consider changing this to "2 (Scheduled)" for specific test users or environments.

  • Restrict the "Email" Channel: Review your Channel determination rules. You can add a condition that checks for a specific "Output Type" or user role; for sample testing, ensure the channel is forced to "PRINT" or "PREVIEW" only, bypassing the email logic.

  • Use a Dummy Email Address: For the quality/test system, it is a "Clean Core" best practice to use the Email Office configuration to redirect all outgoing emails to a single internal "dummy" address rather than actual customers.

Operational Tip for Clients

In the Schedule Sample Printout pop-up, advise users to click "Display" instead of "Print". "Display" generates a PDF preview without triggering the output management dispatch rules, keeping your customer communication safe.

For long-term governance, I recommend using SAP Cloud ALM to document these output rules and validating the "integration seams" with Tricentis Tosca to ensure that email triggers are disabled during automated testing cycles.

entry.title: Restriction on Batch Determination Based on Manufacturing Date
entry.content:
We are currently operating on SAP S/4HANA 2021, where the business process allows goods issue using movement type 601 through sales orders and outbound deliveries.

However, a system inconsistency has been observed in the batch determination process. Specifically, the system is allowing the creation of sales orders and delivery documents with batch selection even when the batch manufacturing (production) date is later than the transaction date.

For example, a sales transaction is being processed on 01.02.2026, whereas the selected batch has a manufacturing date of 04.02.2026. This indicates that the system is permitting the allocation and picking of stock that is not yet produced, which is logically and operationally incorrect.

This gap can lead to:

  • Incorrect inventory commitments
  • Compliance and audit issues
  • Misalignment between physical stock and system stock
  • Potential financial and legal discrepancies

Business Requirement:

The system must enforce a control to ensure that:

  • Batch determination should not consider or propose any batch where the manufacturing date is greater than the sales order date or delivery creation date.
  • During sales order creation (VA01) and delivery processing (VL01N / batch picking), such future-dated batches must be automatically excluded from selection.
  • The restriction should work consistently across all relevant processes including ATP, batch determination, and goods issue.

Expected Outcome:

  • Only batches with a manufacturing date less than or equal to the transaction date should be eligible for selection.
  • The system should prevent users from manually overriding and selecting future-dated batches.
  • This validation should ensure alignment with actual production availability and maintain audit compliance.
